blocks.text: not to be quickly shaken in mind or alarmed, either by spirit or by word or by letter, as though from us, to the effect that the day of the Lord is already here.

2 : False rumors are spreading in writing ( ) and orally ( ; , perhaps indicative of false prophets). clearly indicates that pseudonymous letters were in circulation. is the time of God's apocalyptic judgment (mentioned in 1 Thess 5.2; cf. Isa 13.6; Joel 2.1; Amos 5.18), which still lies in the future.
Let no one deceive you in any way; for that day will not come unless the rebellion comes first and the lawless one is revealed, the one destined for destruction.
3 : In contrast to 1 Thess 5.2–3, the writers note a series of events that will anticipate Jesus's return, including (better, “apostasy”) and indicates a falling away from commitment to God.
He opposes and exalts himself above every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, declaring himself to be God.

3–4 : The (lit., “the man of lawlessness”) is a human person who disobeys God's rules and claims divinity for himself. He is an agent of Satan (v. 9) but not a supernatural being, and not to be conflated with the antichrist(s) of 1 Jn 2.18,22 (cf. 4.3; 2 Jn 7) or the beasts of Rev 13 (esp. v. 18). Yet, as in Revelation, the image of the may refer to a Roman emperor. Clearly the letter audience could identify the referent, even if the modern reader cannot.
Do you not remember that I told you these things when I was still with you?
And you know what is now restraining him, so that he may be revealed when his time comes.
For the mystery of lawlessness is already at work, but only until the one who now restrains it is removed.
6–7 : The implication is that God is ultimately in control by the lawless one until the proper time.
And then the lawless one will be revealed, whom the Lord Jesus will destroy with the breath of his mouth, annihilating him by the manifestation of his coming.
The coming of the lawless one is apparent in the working of Satan, who uses all power, signs, lying wonders,
8–9 : Jesus will destroy the agent of Satan, but not before there is widespread among those who refuse to believe in God. , cf. Job 4.9; Isa 11.4.
and every kind of wicked deception for those who are perishing, because they refused to love the truth and so be saved.
For this reason God sends them a powerful delusion, leading them to believe what is false,
11 : Even in the , God is in control.
so that all who have not believed the truth but took pleasure in unrighteousness will be condemned.
2.1–12: Reassurances about the timing of Jesus's return.

But we must always give thanks to God for you, brothers and sisters beloved by the Lord, because God chose you as the first fruits for salvation through sanctification by the Spirit and through belief in the truth.
13 : Just as God is in control of the events marking the last days (2.1–12), so also those who now believe in the truth (cf. 1 Thess 1.4).
For this purpose he called you through our proclamation of the good news, so that you may obtain the glory of our Lord Jesus Christ.
So then, brothers and sisters,e stand firm and hold fast to the traditions that you were taught by us, either by word of mouth or by our letter.

15 : As a result of being chosen, the Thessalonians must to , i.e., to the things told to them by Paul and his companions verbally and in writing. The may refer to 1 Thessalonians, and is a contrast to the false letter mentioned in 2.2.
Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself and God our Father, who loved us and through grace gave us eternal comfort and good hope,
comfort your hearts and strengthen them in every good work and word.
2.13–17: Second thanksgiving affirming God's faithfulness.
16–17 : As with 1 Thess 3.11–12 (see note there), the invocation shifts the focus of this letter toward practical matters.
